title: Colony Collapse

jokes.text: Trout, a Southern boy who had a Northern man's taste in women.
jokes.setup: The narrator is introducing each member of Gob's entourage with brief character descriptions.
jokes.punchline: A cryptic, innuendo-laden descriptor that implies something about Trout's sexual preferences without spelling it out — the joke is in the confident opacity.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan delivery of an oblique, barely-there joke is very Arrested Development. The economy is excellent — one sentence implies a world of meaning. Not a top-tier joke but a solid character-introduction gag.

jokes.text: J.B.J., a weekend weatherman, all-week party boy and a huge disappointment to his father.
jokes.setup: The narrator introduces another entourage member with the same mock-biographical style.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'weekend weatherman' to 'huge disappointment to his father' lands with a dark, matter-of-fact finality.
jokes.explanation: The 'all-week party boy' flip on 'weekend' is a clean structural joke, and piling on 'huge disappointment to his father' as a final character note is a well-executed gut-punch of understatement.

jokes.text: Oakwood, a studio teacher, who might have had his own entourage, if his part hadn't been cut from the pilot of Modern Family.
jokes.setup: The narrator continues the entourage introductions.
jokes.punchline: The meta reference to Modern Family (a competing ABC single-camera comedy) as the threshold of almost-fame lands as both a joke about celebrity culture and the show's own self-awareness.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'cut from the pilot of Modern Family' is exactly the kind of joke that rewards comedy-savvy viewers. It's also a sly nod to the fact that both shows are similar in format and on the same network, making it meta without being too cute.

jokes.text: And, of course, there was the man they called Getaway.
jokes.setup: After introducing several colourful entourage members, the narrator sets up the reveal of the main character's nickname.
jokes.punchline: The nickname 'Getaway' is a perfect character joke — Gob is a magician who runs away from every commitment, yet he's the designated driver/escape artist for these people.
jokes.explanation: The nickname 'Getaway' works on multiple levels simultaneously: Gob is a magician (escape acts), he's a literal getaway driver for the entourage, and he's emotionally a perpetual runner from commitment. The payoff deepens across the episode. High character integration score is earned.

jokes.text: Gob had come far in the year since his girlfriend was less than an hour from turning 18.
jokes.setup: The narrator sets up a reflection on how far Gob has come in a year.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that his girlfriend was 'less than an hour from turning 18' recontextualizes the 'coming far' as deeply troubling and comedically shocking.
jokes.explanation: This is a classic AD structure: the narrator delivers a seemingly positive note, then the specific detail completely subverts it into something dark. 'Less than an hour from turning 18' is the kind of precision that makes AD fans pause and then burst out laughing. Dark subversive humor executed with precision.

jokes.text: While we run the clock down on this thing, why don't you just go change into something a little less unflattering.
jokes.setup: Gob is with his girlfriend, who is about to turn 18.
jokes.punchline: 'Something a little less unflattering' is a backhanded compliment that reveals Gob's spectacular obliviousness — he's both trying to be romantic and insulting her simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: Very Gob — he thinks he's being suave and romantic but the language reveals he finds her unattractive. 'A little less unflattering' is the double-negative that exposes his self-absorption. Good character-comedy writing.

jokes.text: Ann less-unflattered herself up.
jokes.setup: Gob asks Ann to change into something less unflattering.
jokes.punchline: The narrator adopts Gob's mangled syntax, turning 'less unflattering' into a verb: 'less-unflattered herself up.' This is the narrator-as-character joke AD does brilliantly.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan absorption of Gob's idiotic phrasing into narration-as-fact is a signature AD meta-move. 'Less-unflattered herself up' is a perfectly constructed absurdist callback that only works because we heard the original line. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: You're lucky that I'm chasing after our girlfriend, or I'd have to flatten you!
jokes.setup: George Michael confronts Gob over stealing Ann.
jokes.punchline: The threat is hilariously wimpy — 'I'd have to flatten you' — and undercut by the fact that he's literally chasing after her himself, which he acknowledges while threatening Gob.
jokes.explanation: George Michael's impotent tough-guy posturing is characteristically funny — 'flatten you' is an endearingly un-intimidating verb. Good character work even if it doesn't quite land as a standalone joke.

jokes.text: Way to plant, Ann. You told me George Michael knew about us. Well, he does now, my darling plant. Ann.
jokes.setup: Gob accuses Ann of setting him up by not telling George Michael about their relationship. He calls her a 'plant' (a stooge/setup in magic).
jokes.punchline: The word 'plant' is a magic term but also works literally (Ann is famously plant-like in personality), and then Gob/narrator correct the name back to 'Ann' after saying 'plant' — a running gag about Ann being forgettable.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant double-meaning: 'plant' as both magic term (a confederate in an audience) and as a description of Ann's dull, vegetable-like presence. Then the correction 'Ann' reinforces the running gag that nobody can remember her. Layered and earned.

jokes.text: And after I dazzle everyone tonight on the Queen Mary, my magic career will take off as surely as there's a mouse behind your ear. Well, as surely as there's a mouse behind your ear.
jokes.setup: Gob tries to make a grand proclamation about his career using a magic metaphor.
jokes.punchline: He attempts a coin-behind-ear trope but with a mouse — which is both gross and a setup for the later 'Mice-a-laneous' act — then stumbles on the delivery, undercutting his own bravado.
jokes.explanation: Replacing 'coin' with 'mouse' is a perfect Gob-ism — it's wrong in every way but delivered with complete sincerity. Also foreshadows the Mice-a-laneous act reveal. The repetition ('Well, as surely...') captures Gob's awkward self-correction.

jokes.text: A poll would confirm she was far from alone. For the fifth year in a row, your least favorite entertainers are magicians. A close second, talk show side-kicks. Hmm. You, too, America. 'cause I quit.
jokes.setup: Ann expresses displeasure with magic; the narrator notes a poll would confirm she's not alone.
jokes.punchline: The poll reveals magicians are America's least favorite entertainers for the fifth straight year, talk show sidekicks are second — then Gob's response is 'You, too, America. 'cause I quit' — a defiant, petulant non-sequitur.
jokes.explanation: The escalating specificity — fifth year in a row, talk show sidekicks second — builds beautifully, and Gob's response ('You, too, America. 'cause I quit.') is a perfect character beat: petulant, self-important, and utterly Gob. The structure is clean and the payoff earns its setup.

jokes.text: Gob: 'You, too, America. 'Cause I quit.'
jokes.setup: America has been polled as hating magicians for five years running
jokes.punchline: Gob's response to learning the entire country dislikes him is a defiant 'you too' and an immediate announcement that he quits — only to immediately reverse course
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gob character comedy — his response to nationwide rejection is petulant defiance. 'You too, America' is highly quotable and captures his narcissistic self-pity in three words. Strong economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Of course, Ann's concept of fourth base was very different than Gob's.
jokes.setup: Ann demands Gob be faithful before 'getting to fourth base.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator's aside reveals that Ann's concept of fourth base is religious/spiritual (likely prayer or marriage) while Gob's is sexual — the contrast is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The narrator-as-omniscient-observer deploying a quiet aside to comment on character worldviews is a classic AD move. The implication that Ann's fourth base is something religious/pure (while Gob's is graphic) is funny through character contrast alone.

jokes.text: You think I can't keep it in my pants in front of a bunch of doggy old women and my family? Don't be stupid. Who am I gonna hit on, stupid? My sister? Lindsay? Don't be stupid. She's my sister, that's gross.
jokes.setup: Gob promises to be faithful to Ann for the Queen Mary party.
jokes.punchline: His increasingly defensive 'Don't be stupid' repetition, combined with him specifically naming his sister Lindsay as someone he won't hit on — which he has to clarify is gross — reveals that it's perhaps not as obvious as it should be.
jokes.explanation: The escalating self-defense ('Don't be stupid' x3) combined with the fact that Gob feels he has to specifically exclude his sister from his list of potential conquests — and then has to clarify why — is a masterclass in character-based dark comedy. The repetition builds perfectly.

jokes.text: Now, the story of a family whose future was abruptly canceled, and the one son who had no choice but to keep himself together. It's Gob's Arrested Development.
jokes.setup: The season 4 Arrested Development structure has each episode reframed around a different character, using a variant of the classic opening narration.
jokes.punchline: The show's own title becomes the joke — 'arrested development' applied to Gob specifically, the most emotionally stunted member of the family, while also referencing the show's own cancellation history.
jokes.explanation: Season 4's device of attributing the title to each character individually is a brilliant structural meta-joke. For Gob specifically — 'the one son who had no choice but to keep himself together' is darkly ironic — it resonates perfectly. The show commenting on its own cancellation ('abruptly canceled') as backstory is fourth-wall comedy at its best.

jokes.text: It seemed the only 'her' Gob would be getting off that day was the Queen Mary.
jokes.setup: The narrator transitions from Gob's failed magic show setup to the Queen Mary ship.
jokes.punchline: 'Getting her off' as a double entendre — the Queen Mary (the ship) and a sexual reference — delivered with the narrator's perfect deadpan is a classic AD dirty pun.
jokes.explanation: The double entendre is clean and well-timed. The narrator delivering it with complete seriousness is the key — it's not winking at the audience but rather trusting them to catch it. Good economy.

jokes.text: Two drowned white doves, for 'flowers to doves.' This was a rabbit for 'doves to rabbit,' also drowned. These were mice... For 'rabbit to mice.' No, that can't be done.
jokes.setup: Gob is listing drowned animals from his failed magic act to an insurance agent.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from doves to rabbit to mice is absurd enough, but the agent's flat 'No, that can't be done' — refusing to accept the magical sequence as fact — is the punchline. The orderly insurance-claim framing of a magic disaster is inherently comedic.
jokes.explanation: The insurance claim framing is a perfect comedic device — bureaucratic normalcy applied to absurd magical destruction. 'No, that can't be done' as a flat rejection of the trick's logic (rather than the claim itself) is a beautifully understated punchline. The escalating animals and their causes of death (all drowned) builds wonderfully.

jokes.text: 'Mice-a-laneous.' 'Mouse in purse.' 'Mouse in drink.' 'Here's a mouse, now it's gone.' How'd he do that?
jokes.setup: Gob continues listing his magic props that were destroyed.
jokes.punchline: 'Mice-a-laneous' (Miscellaneous + mice) is a perfect pun name for an act, followed by actual trick names that are comically mundane ('Mouse in drink'), and then Gob's own 'How'd he do that?' self-congratulation on a trick he's describing to an insurance agent.
jokes.explanation: 'Mice-a-laneous' is a brilliant pun — 'miscellaneous' mapped onto 'mice' — and the trick names ('Mouse in drink,' 'Here's a mouse, now it's gone') are hilariously banal for magic. Then Gob breaks to admire his own trick mid-insurance claim with 'How'd he do that?' — classic Gob narcissism. This is among the episode's funniest moments.

jokes.text: Gob repeatedly asking George Michael 'But are we good?' despite George Michael saying 'No' each time
jokes.setup: Gob needs George Michael's blessing to continue dating Ann
jokes.punchline: Gob ignores each 'No' and keeps asking 'But are we good?' as if the answer will change through sheer repetition, revealing his total inability to process rejection
jokes.explanation: The escalating pattern of Gob asking, being denied, and asking again with the same inflection is a masterclass in character-based repetition comedy. Every repeated 'But are we good?' reveals more about Gob's narcissism — he simply cannot accept that 'No' is the final answer. The structure escalates perfectly.

jokes.text: Are we good? No. No, how could we possibly be? But... But are we good? No! You stole my girlfriend. That's a tough thing, and, you know, sometimes you have to ask yourself, 'Are we good?' Is it over between you guys? No. But are we good?
jokes.setup: Gob approaches George Michael to patch things up so he can date Ann.
jokes.punchline: Gob keeps short-circuiting the conversation by returning to 'Are we good?' — completely unable to process that George Michael's 'no' is the actual answer, searching for absolution without doing any of the emotional work.
jokes.explanation: This extended bit is a perfect portrait of Gob's emotional shorthand. He cannot hear 'no' — he just keeps asking the same question hoping a different answer will emerge. The rhythm of the repetition and George Michael's increasingly exasperated responses build beautifully. High character integration because only Gob could be this obliviously selfish.

jokes.text: I mean, yes, we are good... There it is! There it is! Ah! From a nephew to his uncle, and just the 'yes,' the much-vaunted 'yes,' that he gets. Look at you, full of 'yes.' Look at how much 'yes' is in you!
jokes.setup: George Michael, exhausted, accidentally says 'yes' in the process of saying he doesn't know what Gob wants to hear.
jokes.punchline: Gob seizes on the accidental 'yes' with an eruption of self-congratulation — 'Look at how much yes is in you!' — completely overriding the context in which it was said.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comic high point in the first act. Gob's explosion of 'Look at how much yes is in you!' is an instant quotable line. The structure is perfect — the setup (repeated 'Are we good?') makes the payoff proportionally enormous. The specific phrase 'much-vaunted yes' is a masterpiece of character voice. High marks across the board.

jokes.text: And Gob realized there was finally nothing standing in the way of a loving, committed relationship with Ann. My life is a fallacy. ♪ It's just a fallacy ♪
jokes.setup: After getting the 'yes' he needed, the narrator describes Gob's realization about Ann.
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately sings 'My life is a fallacy' — clearly not understanding what 'fallacy' means, likely meaning 'fantasy.' The show lets him sing it without correcting him.
jokes.explanation: Gob misusing 'fallacy' for 'fantasy' while singing it earnestly is perfect character comedy. The show trusts viewers to catch the malapropism without flagging it. The irony that 'my life is a fallacy' is actually accurate for a different reason adds a meta layer.

jokes.text: So, that night, as he broke into her house to break up with her...
jokes.setup: The narrator transitions to Gob visiting Ann.
jokes.punchline: 'Broke into her house to break up with her' — the casual juxtaposition of criminal trespass with romantic cowardice, delivered as if perfectly normal.
jokes.explanation: The narrative compression — breaking and entering as merely a delivery method for a breakup — is a quality AD joke. The economy of 'broke in to break up' has an accidental alliterative elegance too.

jokes.text: Gob! For a second, I thought that was a real guy.
jokes.setup: Gob enters/appears at Ann's home after breaking in.
jokes.punchline: Ann's first reaction is relief that he's not a real intruder — implying she thought the person who broke into her home was a mannequin or prop, which suggests something about either Gob's quality of appearance or Ann's poor threat assessment.
jokes.explanation: A quick, solid laugh — Ann's reaction implies Gob appeared ghost-like or mannequin-like, adding to his general unreality. Good but not exceptional.

jokes.text: But as she unzipped her pajamas, it reminded him of past situations which he'd successfully been aroused. Well, I could wait till after.
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to break up with Ann but she starts undressing.
jokes.punchline: The narrator explains that the sight reminds him of arousal, and Gob's response is 'Well, I could wait till after' — deciding to postpone the breakup for sex, delivered with the show's trademark detached clinical narration.
jokes.explanation: The clinical narration ('situations which he'd successfully been aroused') followed by Gob's immediate capitulation to hormones over principles is classic Gob. The understatement in the narration elevates what could be a cheap joke.

jokes.text: So how did you like your egg? I said you were fine.
jokes.setup: The morning after, Ann asks how Gob liked his egg (singular).
jokes.punchline: Gob responds to a question about food ('How did you like your egg?') by reviewing the sex ('I said you were fine') — completely mishearing or recontextualizing the question, while also delivering a crushingly backhanded sexual review.
jokes.explanation: A two-line exchange that works on multiple levels: 'egg' (singular, Ann's austerity), Gob mishearing the question as a sexual review, and the devastating critical assessment of 'I said you were fine.' The economy is near-perfect — maximum comedy per word. One of the episode's best quiet jokes.

jokes.text: Was there something you wanted to talk about when you came in through my window?
jokes.setup: Ann asks casually about Gob's arrival method.
jokes.punchline: She treats breaking and entering as a completely normal and unremarkable point of entry, suggesting this is not their first rodeo with unusual arrival methods — or that Ann is so passive she doesn't register crimes against her.
jokes.explanation: Ann's casual acceptance of window-entry is a good character beat, reinforcing her general lack of self-preservation and emotional affect. Solid but not outstanding.

jokes.text: Gob's extended stammering attempt to break up with Ann while she interrogates him.
jokes.setup: Gob tries to articulate that he wants to break up, while Ann's simple questions reduce him to incoherence.
jokes.punchline: The extended stammer ('It's the... The questions... You have so many of these...') collapses into complete gibberish, culminating in a nonsensical fragment about dollar amounts and bath robes ('In the $32... In the $3,400...') — the joke is Gob's complete inability to function emotionally.
jokes.explanation: The extended cringe comedy of Gob's emotional paralysis — reduced to fragments about price tags mid-thought — is a masterclass in character-based discomfort humor. Less quotable than other jokes but high in cringe impact. The visual of Gob clearly imploding would be extremely funny to watch.

jokes.text: Well, marry me. Yes. Yes, I'll marry you, Gob! Of course, Gob meant it in the showbizzy way his niece had always used when she was accused of being too young. What are you, like 15? Marry me. Marry me. Marry me.
jokes.setup: Gob, trying to escape the awkward breakup conversation, accidentally blurts 'marry me.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator explains he meant it in the 'showbizzy way' his niece Maeby uses — as a dismissive expression — then cuts to the flashback of Maeby's famous 'Marry me!' bit, showing the exact comedic register Gob was going for.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Maeby's running gag is perfectly deployed — explaining that Gob meant a proposal the way his teenage niece uses it as a deflection is both a great character moment and a wonderful cross-episode running gag payoff. 'But like many evangelicals, Ann took it literally' is a brilliant additional layer.

jokes.text: Gob was surrounded by unconditional love from a family for the first time in his life. [Beat of silence/reaction]
jokes.setup: Ann's evangelical family celebrates the engagement with genuine warmth.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's note that this is 'the first time in his life' Gob has experienced unconditional family love is devastating — and the implied reaction beat of Gob processing this in the midst of a mistake he made by accident is the visual joke.
jokes.explanation: The emotional gut-punch here is real — Gob, the unloved middle child, accidentally finds unconditional love from strangers through a mistake. The narrator's clinical delivery makes it funnier and sadder simultaneously. AD's ability to layer genuine pathos into comedy is on full display.

jokes.text: I've made a huge mistake.
jokes.setup: Gob is surrounded by Ann's loving evangelical family celebrating his accidental proposal.
jokes.punchline: 'I've made a huge mistake' — Gob's signature catchphrase, delivered in context perfectly.
jokes.explanation: 'I've made a huge mistake' is one of the most iconic lines in the entire series. Its deployment here is perfectly timed — arriving just as the narrator tells us Gob is experiencing unconditional love for the first time. The contrast between the warmth he's receiving and his horror at his situation is the full joke. Originality is lower because it's a catchphrase, but impact is enormous.

jokes.text: I'm sure Gob helped himself to the money. Hey, I got mouths to feed. Mouths? Mouth. Her. Oh, hey, mouth. I didn't see you sitting there. We rode up in the elevator together. I'm blanking.
jokes.setup: Gob introduces Ann at a family meeting.
jokes.punchline: The running gag of no one acknowledging or remembering Ann's existence — 'I'm blanking' after being told they rode up in the elevator together — is a direct callback to the 'Her?' tradition.
jokes.explanation: The evolution of 'Her?' into 'I'm blanking' after riding in an elevator together is a good escalation of the running gag. 'Oh, hey, mouth' is also a quietly funny line — acknowledging her only by her function.

jokes.text: Yeah, me and Blank are getting the old Christian magic act back together. I mean, you don't expect people to actually believe that I'm Jesus if I'm walking around in rags.
jokes.setup: Gob announces he's reviving his Christian magic act with Ann for the wedding.
jokes.punchline: Calling Ann 'Blank' (literally unable to remember her name), then his concern about costume credibility for impersonating Jesus — 'you don't expect people to believe I'm Jesus in rags' — treats the blasphemy as a production problem.
jokes.explanation: The 'Blank' name is a good extension of the running gag, and the costume concern ('rags') as the primary obstacle to Jesus credibility is delightfully Gob.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder's making a fortune with that gay magician act. Gob's long-time nemesis had come out of the closet and used it to great advantage in his act.
jokes.setup: Gob explains why he needs a gimmick like everyone else.
jokes.punchline: Tony Wonder coming out as gay and immediately commercializing it for his magic act — 'used it to great advantage' — is a sharp joke about the commodification of identity and celebrity.
jokes.explanation: This setup pays off enormously later ('I'm here, I'm queer, and now I'm in a speaker') and is also part of Tony Wonder's larger Season 4 arc. Good foreshadowing delivered as a quick joke.

jokes.text: Who's getting married? Her! Who's marrying her? Me. Did I not open with that? You sure didn't.
jokes.setup: Gob announces his wedding to the family.
jokes.punchline: The family's 'Her?' instinct resurfaces ('Who's marrying her? Me.') followed by Gob being genuinely surprised he forgot to mention the wedding in his opening, and the family's dry 'You sure didn't.'
jokes.explanation: Good Bluth family dynamic — the dry disbelief that Gob would bury the lede on his own engagement. Efficient comedy.

jokes.text: My lovely Ann... Who? My daughter. Oh, I didn't know you had a daughter. You've met her several times. Oh! She's sitting right next to you. Oh! That's why she's there. I thought you were hair.
jokes.setup: Pastor Veal introduces Ann on the TV show.
jokes.punchline: Father Marsala has met Ann multiple times but doesn't register her presence at all — culminating in 'I thought you were hair,' which is both an insult to Ann's hair and a testament to her total invisibility as a person.
jokes.explanation: 'I thought you were hair' is an outstanding extension of the Ann invisibility running gag. The escalation — from not remembering her, to not seeing her sitting next to him, to mistaking her for hair — is perfectly calibrated. One of the best deployments of this gag.

jokes.text: At our wedding, I will be performing one of my famous magical illusions. That once your eyes have beholden it, you will put no God before me, because of its spectacularity.
jokes.setup: Gob announces his wedding magic trick on the religious TV show.
jokes.punchline: 'You will put no God before me, because of its spectacularity' — on a religious TV show, Gob accidentally claims to be superior to God, then uses the non-word 'spectacularity' to cap it.
jokes.explanation: Gob claiming superiority to God on a religious television program — and the show's hosts presumably just sitting there — is a great piece of character comedy. 'Spectacularity' as a non-word delivered with total confidence is the cherry on top.

jokes.text: Love for and God. Ann. And God.
jokes.setup: Gob tries to clarify that his trick is out of love for Ann AND God.
jokes.punchline: He mangles the order ('Love for and God'), then corrects himself to 'Ann' — again demonstrating that Ann's name is perpetually an afterthought or placeholder.
jokes.explanation: A quick, efficient running-gag deployment. The syntax collapse of 'Love for and God' is a Gob-characteristic verbal stumble.

jokes.text: Gob was feeling bolder, which was perhaps why Michael returned home to find this. [Gob is nailed to a cross in the living room]
jokes.setup: The narrator sets up the scene transition.
jokes.punchline: The pun 'feeling bolder' (bolder/boulder) combined with the visual of Gob crucified in Michael's living room — the audacity of the physical setup.
jokes.explanation: 'Bolder/boulder' is an excellent AD-style pun embedded in narration, and the visual payoff — Gob literally on a cross in Michael's home — is a great physical comedy set piece. The narrator's understatement ('which was perhaps why Michael returned home to find this') makes the reveal land harder.

jokes.text: And yea, as if to be arisen the third or fourth day with all the magic of... Jesus Christ! Sorry. Wasn't on there too good.
jokes.setup: Gob is practicing his Jesus resurrection illusion on a cross in Michael's living room.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'Jesus Christ!' exclamation in response to finding Gob on the cross is both a genuine expletive and literally what Gob is imitating — and Gob responds to the swear as if it's part of the bit ('Sorry. Wasn't on there too good').
jokes.explanation: Exceptional wordplay — Michael's natural expletive ('Jesus Christ!') lands in a scene about Jesus, and Gob's apologetic response ('Wasn't on there too good') treats it as a performance review. The double-meaning and timing are pristine. One of the episode's best constructed moments.

jokes.text: Yeah, what's the illusion? That you actually love the bride? Hey, that's good patter.
jokes.setup: Michael skeptically asks about Gob's illusion for the wedding.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'That you actually love the bride?' is a cutting burn — then Gob's response 'Hey, that's good patter' (magic terminology for stage banter) completely deflects the emotional wound by recasting the insult as a professional compliment.
jokes.explanation: Michael's burn lands perfectly, then Gob's 'Hey, that's good patter' is a double-layer joke: using magic terminology to deflect emotional pain while inadvertently confirming Michael's accusation. The fact that Gob takes it as a professional compliment rather than an insult is peak character comedy. Near-exceptional.

jokes.text: Except for I need people to root for the Jesus character.
jokes.setup: Michael's 'good patter' actually isn't good for Gob's illusion.
jokes.punchline: Gob's concern is not theological but theatrical — he needs audience sympathy for 'the Jesus character' as if Jesus is a fictional role he's developing, not a religious figure.
jokes.explanation: Gob treating Jesus as 'the Jesus character' — a dramatic role requiring audience sympathy — is a great character moment. The blasphemy is accidental and therefore funnier.

jokes.text: I don't remember a biblical passage where Jesus came out of a boulder. Is it in there? Oh, no, no, no, the boulder's my escape plan. They think I'm somewhere else. I'm actually in the boulder.
jokes.setup: Michael questions the biblical accuracy of Gob's trick.
jokes.punchline: Gob's sincere question 'Is it in there?' about whether the boulder is in the Bible, followed by the reveal that he's using the 'cave' as a literal escape route — 'They think I'm somewhere else. I'm actually in the boulder' — treats magic misdirection and the resurrection as synonymous.
jokes.explanation: Gob conflating magic trick mechanics with theological events is a sustained running joke in this episode that never gets old. 'Is it in there?' delivered earnestly is a good beat.

jokes.text: I have a bee company. You stole my idea? Uh-huh, I did not steal your... We sell... It would take hours.
jokes.setup: Michael announces his new company; Gob reveals he also started a bee company.
jokes.punchline: Gob claiming Michael stole his idea (bees), then immediately failing to explain the business model — 'We sell... It would take hours' — suggests there is no coherent business model.
jokes.explanation: 'We sell... It would take hours' is a perfect joke about Gob having no actual business plan. The callback to the Michael B. Company / bee storyline running through season 4 adds context.

jokes.text: My bees are dropping like flies, and I need them to fly like bees.
jokes.setup: Gob explains why his bee business is struggling.
jokes.punchline: 'My bees are dropping like flies' is a standard idiom used literally (bees dying), then inverted: 'I need them to fly like bees' — the correction of the mixed metaphor becomes the joke.
jokes.explanation: An outstanding piece of wordplay. 'Dropping like flies' applied to bees is funny enough, but 'I need them to fly like bees' as the correction — flipping the idiom back on itself — is genuinely brilliant. Perfect economy. One of the best pure wordplay jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: What if I don't actually get married? I don't think you'd need a best man, then, right? No, I guess, at that point, it'd really be more assistant.
jokes.setup: Gob floats the idea of not going through with the wedding while asking Michael to be best man/assistant.
jokes.punchline: Michael points out that without a wedding, Gob wouldn't need a best man — Gob agrees but notes he'd still need an assistant (for the magic act), completely prioritizing the illusion over the marriage.
jokes.explanation: The reveal that even if the wedding were canceled, the magic act would still require an assistant — making the wedding optional but the trick non-negotiable — is perfect Gob characterization.

jokes.text: Marriage is a lot of pressure, and she's not into it. Meanwhile, I'm working out like crazy, my diet has become insane, and this Jesus character... I mean, he was shredded.
jokes.setup: Gob explains his concerns about the wedding.
jokes.punchline: He pivots from relationship concerns to his fitness regimen to justify it by noting Jesus 'was shredded' — treating physical preparation for impersonating Jesus as a primary marital concern.
jokes.explanation: 'He was shredded' applied to Jesus — delivered with the sincerity of a man genuinely concerned about meeting a physical benchmark — is a great Gob moment. The pivot from marriage to Jesus's physique is the classic Gob misdirection.

jokes.text: Meanwhile, we have sex one time, and then she's just, you know, over it. Lets herself go. Her stomach's out to here.
jokes.setup: Gob continues complaining about Ann's behavior after sex.
jokes.punchline: The implication that Ann is pregnant — 'Her stomach's out to here' — is delivered as a complaint about her 'letting herself go,' completely oblivious to what this means.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels: Gob complaining about Ann's body while inadvertently describing pregnancy symptoms (which he seems not to register), the dark implication that she's pregnant from their one encounter, and his complete self-absorption preventing him from connecting the dots. The misdirection is excellent.

jokes.text: Yeah, I know you're in the boulder. But how'd I get in the boulder?
jokes.setup: Gob and Michael's conversation has been interrupted as Michael notices Gob is in the escape boulder.
jokes.punchline: Michael matter-of-factly says 'I know you're in the boulder' — and Gob's response 'But how'd I get in the boulder?' treats his own magic as a mystery he can't solve, admiring his own trick even while he's inside it.
jokes.explanation: Gob being mystified by his own trick — 'But how'd I get in the boulder?' — is a perfect extension of his self-aggrandizing obliviousness. The reveal is also a transition to the physical comedy of the wedding sequence.

jokes.text: I know it's bad luck for you to see me in my gown before the wedding. Well, hopefully I haven't.
jokes.setup: On the wedding day, Ann worries about Gob seeing her in her gown.
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'Well, hopefully I haven't' — delivered with the precise level of uncertainty that implies he honestly cannot remember — is devastating both as a burn and as self-revelation.
jokes.explanation: Economy is exceptional — Gob's uncertainty about whether he's already seen his bride on the wedding day tells us everything about the relationship in six words. The deadpan delivery would make it even funnier in execution.

jokes.text: I didn't want to make a big deal about it. You haven't.
jokes.setup: Gob claims he didn't want to make a big deal about his wedding.
jokes.punchline: Ann's dry 'You haven't' — confirming that no one has shown up and nobody cares — is an unexpectedly sharp retort from the usually passive Ann.
jokes.explanation: Two words from Ann that serve as an unexpectedly sharp punchline. The economy is near-perfect, and it's funnier coming from Ann, who is typically the straight-man victim of the humor rather than its deliverer.

jokes.text: I'm playing Roman Centurion Number Two. What are you playing? I'm the groom.
jokes.setup: Tobias shows up at Gob's wedding as a background actor, not a guest.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's question 'What are you playing?' — directed at the actual groom — perfectly captures his inability to separate performance from reality, while also being an inadvertent burn on Gob's authenticity as a husband.
jokes.explanation: Tobias asking the groom 'What are you playing?' is a classic Tobias moment — his actor's worldview means he sees everyone as performing. The payoff 'I'm the groom' lands perfectly because it confirms the wedding is indistinguishable from a production. High character integration for both Tobias and Gob.

jokes.text: Father Marsala's John the Baptist. Then the antiabortion drama, Embryo Dan: It Would Have Been a Wonderful Life. And later, break out the bagels. It's time for Father M's lighthearted comedy, A Jew Came to Dinner.
jokes.setup: A promo reel for the Miracle Network's programming is shown.
jokes.punchline: The shows escalate from parody biblical drama to an anti-abortion drama with a punny title ('Embryo Dan: It Would Have Been a Wonderful Life') to the deeply uncomfortable 'A Jew Came to Dinner' — a Father Marsala vehicle.
jokes.explanation: 'Embryo Dan: It Would Have Been a Wonderful Life' is a superb title parody — It's a Wonderful Life mapped onto an anti-abortion drama. 'A Jew Came to Dinner' is genuinely audacious dark comedy. The escalation of the fake show titles is expert comedy construction.

jokes.text: I hate to be the guy who quotes his own reviews, but His Word magazine called my Jew 'pitiful.'
jokes.setup: Tobias cites critical response to his performance in 'A Jew Came to Dinner.'
jokes.punchline: 'His Word magazine called my Jew pitiful' is a sentence that makes no grammatical sense outside of context and complete sense within it, capturing Tobias's theatrical self-regard and the show's absurdist world.
jokes.explanation: 'His Word magazine called my Jew pitiful' is one of the episode's most quotable lines. Tobias citing a bad review with the defensiveness of someone who hates quoting bad reviews, about a role in a show called 'A Jew Came to Dinner,' for a publication called 'His Word' — every noun is funny. Near-exceptional.

jokes.text: It's true, this is a magical trick. If what Jesus did was a trick. I say it wasn't. It was an illusion.
jokes.setup: Gob begins his wedding magic act in front of the congregation.
jokes.punchline: Gob draws the magician's distinction between 'trick' and 'illusion' as a theological argument about the Resurrection — a breathtaking piece of character-specific absurdity.
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best jokes. Gob's professional obsession with the trick/illusion distinction — established as a running gag across the series — gets applied here to Jesus's resurrection. The theological implications are staggering in the best absurdist way: Gob is defending Jesus's artistry by insisting the resurrection was 'an illusion.' Character integration is nearly perfect.

jokes.text: Ooh! They did not like that one.
jokes.setup: The congregation reacts negatively to Gob's claim that Jesus performed an illusion.
jokes.punchline: Gob's breezy meta-commentary on the audience reaction — 'Ooh! They did not like that one' — treating a religious congregation's horror as standard performance feedback.
jokes.explanation: Gob treating the congregation's theological outrage as performer's audience feedback is a great character beat. The self-awareness within his total obliviousness is very AD.

jokes.text: I am not the real Jesus. I am the Amazing Jesus!
jokes.setup: Gob performs at the wedding, trying to distinguish himself from the actual Jesus.
jokes.punchline: 'The Amazing Jesus!' — Gob rebranding Jesus the way a magician gets a stage name — implying the real Jesus exists but Gob is the superior, more marketable version.
jokes.explanation: Gob's magician branding instinct applied to Jesus — 'the Amazing Jesus' as if Jesus needed a stage name upgrade — is a perfect joke. The confidence of the announcement and the congregation's implied horror make this one of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Yes, the real Jesus came off the cross and went into his cave a dead man.
jokes.setup: Gob narrates the 'plot' of the Resurrection for his magic trick.
jokes.punchline: Gob's matter-of-fact biblical narration reducing the resurrection to magic-trick mechanics — 'the real Jesus came off the cross and went into his cave a dead man' — treats the foundation of Christianity as a stage setup.
jokes.explanation: Sustained dark comedy that works because Gob's irreverence is completely sincere — he's not trying to offend, he's literally reducing the Resurrection to trick mechanics.

jokes.text: Pastor Veal, let me remind you that you are in a church in front of your daughter, your congregation, God. You cannot tell a lie. Right, nothing out of the ordinary? No, I didn't see anything.
jokes.setup: Gob asks Pastor Veal to enter the boulder and confirm there's no escape route, invoking God and the congregation as witnesses against lying.
jokes.punchline: Pastor Veal — who clearly sees the escape mechanism — says 'No, I didn't see anything,' directly lying to God while standing in a church, having just been reminded by Gob that he cannot lie.
jokes.explanation: The irony is perfectly constructed: Gob invokes the most powerful anti-lying framework possible (God, church, congregation, daughter), and Pastor Veal immediately lies anyway. The complicity of a pastor in a magic trick — and his instant capitulation — is a sharp piece of religious satire.

jokes.text: I'm not going to lock myself in a cave for three days before this wedding ceremony. No, no. No, no, no. I plan on beating his record by two full weeks!
jokes.setup: Gob explains he won't be in the boulder for three days like Jesus.
jokes.punchline: The 'no, no' sounds like the setup for a reasonable alternative — then 'two full weeks' as the escalation is a perfect Gob move: not less extreme, but far more extreme for no clear reason.
jokes.explanation: The fake-out structure is immaculate: 'I'm not going to lock myself in a cave for three days' — the congregation and viewers expect a sensible alternative — 'I plan on beating his record by two full weeks.' The escalation perfectly encapsulates Gob: he cannot do things in moderation, even his mistakes are grandiose. Excellent structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Well, I guess we'll wait two weeks and see if he's in there. Is that okay with you, Ann? He's not coming back.
jokes.setup: Gob has been sealed in the boulder, knocked unconscious.
jokes.punchline: The crowd accepts waiting two weeks as a perfectly normal option for a wedding, and Ann's flat 'He's not coming back' — delivered as stone-cold prophecy — perfectly captures both her resignation and her read on Gob.
jokes.explanation: 'He's not coming back' becomes a brilliant running refrain through the episode — Ann's cold certainty doubles as a joke about Gob's relationship with commitment and as a literal physical observation. It will be echoed later, making this a setup for callbacks.

jokes.text: Hey, kids. [Gob inside the boulder in the parking lot as children play bingo inside the church]
jokes.setup: Gob is stuck inside a fake boulder in the church parking lot.
jokes.punchline: The visual of Gob weakly calling 'Hey, kids' from inside a boulder in a parking lot while life continues normally around him is absurdist physical comedy.
jokes.explanation: The image of Gob trapped in a parking lot boulder calling out to children is a sustained absurdist visual gag. Works well as a status-drop joke — America's Amazing Jesus is calling 'Hey, kids' from a fake rock.

jokes.text: Eleven exciting days, although nothing's really happened. Day 11. Once he gets out, it's going to be a beautiful, beautiful wedding. He's not coming back.
jokes.setup: The church community provides updates on Gob's progress in the boulder.
jokes.punchline: The juxtaposition of the buoyant church member ('beautiful, beautiful wedding') with Ann's cold 'He's not coming back' repeated as a running refrain, and the narrator's understatement that 'nothing's really happened' in 11 days.
jokes.explanation: Ann's refrain 'He's not coming back' gains comedic weight through repetition, especially contrasted with the optimistic church members. The running gag of no one rescuing Gob is sustained absurdist comedy.

jokes.text: And a hoarse, weakened Gob was unable to stop them as the boulder was shipped to a storage unit in Tustin. Although a keen eye could see that Gob was alive but not well.
jokes.setup: Gob's boulder is being removed because the church wants the space.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'a keen eye could see that Gob was alive but not well' — as the boulder is casually shipped off to Tustin like old furniture — is a masterpiece of deadpan understatement applied to a man being accidentally shipped to a storage unit.
jokes.explanation: The narrator delivering 'alive but not well' with perfect equanimity — as if this is an acceptable status update — is premium AD narrator comedy. The specificity of 'Tustin' adds to the joke (a mundane suburban location for this absurd fate).

jokes.text: And that's why this particular chair was empty at the trial of Lucille Bluth.
jokes.setup: The narrator explains why Gob missed a family event.
jokes.punchline: The narrator ties Gob's boulder imprisonment to his absence from Lucille's trial — connecting the absurd storyline to another thread in the season's tapestry with characteristic AD efficiency.
jokes.explanation: A solid narrative connector joke — the payoff is retroactive (the empty chair at the trial was set up elsewhere in the season). Rewatching adds value but the joke itself is more connective tissue than comedy.

jokes.text: There's a note. 'If I have not yet returned, I am in the Rapture.' Oh, and then it says... What does he think we've been doing?
jokes.setup: The boulder is empty and someone finds a note from Gob.
jokes.punchline: Gob's note claiming he's been 'Raptured' as his cover story is immediately undercut by Ann's dry 'What does he think we've been doing?' — which implies the congregation has been waiting for the Rapture/his return anyway, making his excuse meaningless.
jokes.explanation: Gob claiming to have been Raptured is great character-specific excuse-making, and Ann's rejoinder — that the congregation has literally been waiting for the Rapture, so his excuse doesn't distinguish itself — is a sharp layered joke. Good structural payoff.

jokes.text: Moment of truth, people. It's a masker. Jesus, there's a man in there. Pack, stack, and all that. Let's get the bidding started at $100. Anybody? Nope. It's hissing.
jokes.setup: An auction reality show opens Gob's storage unit, which contains the boulder.
jokes.punchline: The auctioneer's discovery of 'a man in there' is treated as just another item — 'Pack, stack, and all that' — and Gob's apparent hissing from inside starts the bidding at $100, with no takers.
jokes.explanation: The storage auction parody is excellent — 'Jesus, there's a man in there' treated as part of the auction catalog, and then 'nobody bids on the hissing man-boulder' is a tremendous status-drop for Gob. The specific 'Nope' from the crowd is a perfect beat.

jokes.text: Our first fight, and like all fights, you're a little right, I'm a little right.
jokes.setup: Ann confronts Gob about humiliating her and ruining their wedding.
jokes.punchline: Gob's completely inappropriate 'you're a little right, I'm a little right' as a conflict resolution framework — after he's objectively 100% at fault — encapsulates his pathological inability to accept responsibility.
jokes.explanation: Gob's 'you're a little right, I'm a little right' is a perfect encapsulation of his pathological need to be 50% right even when he's 0% right. Classic character comedy that earns its craft score through specificity.

jokes.text: I sold your cave on Craigslist. So it was all worth it. We have money to start our life together.
jokes.setup: Ann tries to find a silver lining in the disaster.
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'So it was all worth it' in response to Ann selling the cave/boulder for money — completely reframing a catastrophic few weeks as successful real estate.
jokes.explanation: 'So it was all worth it' as Gob's conclusion from the cave sale is a perfectly timed line that shows his complete inability to process emotional consequences. Good but not outstanding.

jokes.text: Now we have our nest, Egg... Ann.
jokes.setup: Gob tries to use a romantic metaphor about their 'nest.'
jokes.punchline: He almost calls her 'Egg' — a reference to her nickname among the Bluths — before self-correcting to 'Ann,' reinforcing the running gag of the family calling her 'Egg' and the general difficulty everyone has with her name.
jokes.explanation: 'Egg' as a near-slip is a great callback to the family's nickname for Ann, and the nest/egg metaphor makes the slip even funnier — a nest needs an egg, so calling her Egg in this context is accidentally poetic. Good running-gag deployment.

jokes.text: The Holy... [Gob's extended reaction to Ann's suggestion that he let the Holy Ghost inside him]
jokes.setup: Ann tells Gob he'll be alone unless he lets the Holy Ghost inside him.
jokes.punchline: Gob's stammering reaction to the phrase 'let the Holy Ghost inside you' — clearly processing it as a sexual proposition before his brain catches up — is a pure reaction beat that plays on the double meaning.
jokes.explanation: The pause and stammer from Gob — the audience can see his mind processing the sexual double meaning of 'let it inside you' — is a classic reaction beat. Pure character comedy with good economy.

jokes.text: I'm ready to let the son of Gob enfold me.
jokes.setup: Gob, affected by Ann's words, calls his son Steve to say he's ready to connect.
jokes.punchline: He mishears/misremembering 'Son of God' as 'son of Gob' — both because his name is Gob (a near-homophone of God) and because he's calling his literal son, so 'son of Gob' is accidentally accurate in two ways.
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's finest jokes. 'Son of Gob' works on three simultaneous levels: (1) Gob's name vs. God's name — a near-homophone collapse, (2) He is literally calling his son Steve, making him the actual 'son of Gob,' (3) The religious sincerity of the moment makes the accidental self-reference funnier. Perfect economy, extremely quotable, and uniquely achievable only by this character. This is exceptional AD wordplay.

jokes.text: Have you been to the club And, owned by Jeremy Pivan? I'm gonna be there tonight about 8:00. Maybe you could join me.
jokes.setup: Gob calls his son Steve to emotionally reconnect.
jokes.punchline: Even in his most emotionally sincere moment, Gob can't resist mentioning a club and name-dropping 'Jeremy Pivan' (Jeremy Piven, star of Entourage — the show being parodied by the entourage storyline) as a casual flex.
jokes.explanation: The name 'Jeremy Pivan' (a mangling of Jeremy Piven, star of Entourage) is a subtle meta-joke about the Entourage parody running through the episode. Gob name-dropping him even in a vulnerable moment is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: Nah, he got it. Okay.
jokes.setup: Gob leaves the message, clearly has NOT explained who he is to his son.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'Nah, he got it. Okay.' — confidently asserting that the incomprehensible message landed — is the narrator doing Gob's denial work for him, essentially becoming a co-conspirator in his self-deception.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most distinctive narrator jokes. 'Nah, he got it. Okay.' is the narrator stepping out of objectivity to validate Gob's delusion — essentially telling the audience 'he's wrong but let's not tell him.' The economy is near-perfect. This is a particularly AD-specific form of meta comedy.

jokes.text: Lost my wife, lost my career. When I was in that storage unit at the bottom of that rock, it was like I hit... Rock bottom? No, no, not that. More like a trending downward moment that just... I don't know.
jokes.setup: Gob vents to the bartender about his life falling apart.
jokes.punchline: The bartender offers 'rock bottom' as the phrase Gob is looking for — he literally was at the bottom of a rock — and Gob rejects it, insisting on the more vague 'trending downward moment.' His ego cannot accept even an accurate idiom.
jokes.explanation: A spectacular joke. 'Rock bottom' is offered and literally applicable (inside a rock, at the bottom), and Gob rejects it in favor of 'trending downward moment' — because accepting the phrase would be accepting defeat. The AD double-meaning of 'rock bottom' (literal + idiomatic) and Gob's oblivious rejection is near-perfect comedy construction.

jokes.text: Maybe I'm being tested like that guy 'Jawb' from the Bible. Job. Yes? Job. Yes, sir. Job. Yeah.
jokes.setup: Gob compares himself to the biblical Job.
jokes.punchline: Gob pronounces 'Job' as 'Jawb' — as if it's a modern name — and the bartender corrects him ('Job') while Gob continues to interpret the correction as agreement ('Yes? / Yes, sir. / Yeah.').
jokes.explanation: The 'Jawb/Job' pronunciation exchange is an exceptional piece of misdirection — each correction ('Job') is interpreted by Gob as confirmation ('Yes?'), creating a loop where he never gets corrected because his misunderstanding makes all corrections sound like agreement. Great structure, very quotable.

jokes.text: God, I dated a chick named Eve once. Biggest mistake of my life.
jokes.setup: Gob learns his son's mother's name is Eve, which triggers a personal reflection.
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'Biggest mistake of my life' — oblivious to the fact that this woman is his son's mother, and that dating her produced a child he's now trying to reconnect with.
jokes.explanation: Gob casually dismissing Eve as his biggest mistake while talking to his son by Eve — without connecting the dots — is a good character beat. The irony is perfectly delivered through obliviousness.

jokes.text: I'll be there at 6:45 with two cups of coffee. Oh, Dad, this is the greatest day of my life. No, this is the greatest day of my life.
jokes.setup: Gob and Steve excitedly plan to work together.
jokes.punchline: The competing 'greatest day of my life' claims — the son's is touching, Gob's is character-undermining (his son agreeing to let him come to work is the greatest day of his life, more than his wedding, his career, anything).
jokes.explanation: The mirrored 'greatest day' lines work because Gob's sincerity is earned — he genuinely has no one — and because it makes this low-stakes moment (joining pest control) feel enormous relative to his more dramatic failures. Touchingly funny.

jokes.text: I bet it's been a long time since you've seen your... Cherry. I have lupus.
jokes.setup: Gob shows off a magic trick at the bar — making a cherry appear — to an older woman.
jokes.punchline: Gob's setup 'I bet it's been a long time since you've seen your...' implies a sexual double entendre about to land; the punchline 'Cherry' (the fruit/magic prop) is both the reveal and the target of the woman's 'I have lupus' — which completely derails the bit.
jokes.explanation: 'I have lupus' as a response to a cherry magic trick is a great non-sequitur punchline — the woman's complete non-sequitur deflates Gob's showmanship perfectly. 'Could have warned a guy' adds a nice button.

jokes.text: No, it's an illusion. A trick is something a whore does for... Do a trick and I'll pay for your bar tab.
jokes.setup: Mark Cherry's handler asks Gob to do a magic trick for Mark.
jokes.punchline: Gob begins his famous 'A trick is something a whore does for money' speech — a running gag from earlier seasons — only to be cut off mid-sentence by the offer of a free bar tab, at which point he immediately capitulates.
jokes.explanation: The truncated 'A trick is something a whore does for...' — cut off by the bar tab offer that makes Gob immediately do the trick — is a perfect callback structure. The joke is in how quickly his principles evaporate for free drinks. One of the series' best running gags, deployed perfectly.

jokes.text: That is a David Spade. Check your purse. Oh! Nice.
jokes.setup: Gob performs a magic trick for the crowd, including catching a famous celebrity's attention.
jokes.punchline: Gob does the trick and says 'That is a David Spade' — using the celebrity's name as a descriptor for what he's produced (presumably a dollar bill trick or similar), making David Spade a unit of currency or quality measure.
jokes.explanation: The use of 'David Spade' as a noun for some unit of measure in the trick is a quick, efficient celebrity gag that works better in context. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Never yell 'fire' in a crowded place. It's never funny. That's always funny.
jokes.setup: Rebel Alley yells 'Fire!' during the bar magic show; the narrator explains she's working on a PSA.
jokes.punchline: The narrator says 'It's never funny,' then immediately adds 'That's always funny' — a self-contradicting aside that breaks with the narrator's usual objectivity to express a personal opinion.
jokes.explanation: The narrator expressing a direct personal opinion ('That's always funny') in contradiction to his own narration ('It's never funny') is a rare and effective meta-comedic move. The economy is excellent — two sentences do a lot of work.

jokes.text: Getting away is kind of my specialty. I'll have us out of here in a flash.
jokes.setup: Mark Cherry's team needs to escape paparazzi at the bar.
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'Getting away is kind of my specialty' — a reference to both his escape-act magic career and his perpetual emotional running — delivered with complete earnest confidence, unaware of how literally true it is.
jokes.explanation: The double meaning of 'getting away' — escape magic AND emotional avoidance — is a theme the episode has been building, and this moment crystallizes it perfectly. The self-unawareness is the joke.

jokes.text: He's not coming back. [Steve watches Gob drive past]
jokes.setup: Steve waits for his father as promised.
jokes.punchline: Steve delivers Ann's refrain 'He's not coming back' — the running gag about Gob's inability to follow through now applied to his son, completing the thematic circle.
jokes.explanation: The transfer of Ann's line to Steve is a devastating structural callback — it transforms what was Ann's cold assessment of Gob's character into a son's heartbroken realization about his father. The economy (four words) delivering that emotional punch makes it one of the episode's most powerful jokes. High marks across the board.

jokes.text: The next few months were a blur of partying. And shame.
jokes.setup: The narrator transitions through a montage of Gob's entourage lifestyle.
jokes.punchline: 'And shame.' — the one-word addendum that reframes the entire montage. The pause before it is the key beat.
jokes.explanation: The pause and 'And shame' is a classic AD narrator beat — the addendum that recontextualizes everything that came before. Economy is outstanding.

jokes.text: But Gob had a trick for helping him forget the shame. Unfortunately it also helped him forget that he tried to forget the shame.
jokes.setup: Gob uses forget-me-now pills to erase shameful memories.
jokes.punchline: The narrator establishes the recursive logic: the pill erases the shame, but also erases the memory of taking the pill, which means the shame returns and he takes another pill, creating an infinite loop.
jokes.explanation: The recursive logic of the forget-me-now pill — it erases the memory of taking the pill, creating a shame-loop — is a clever piece of comedic world-building. The setup-and-logical-extension structure is clean.

jokes.text: The roofie circle: Whereby a roofie is taken the day after a degrading event too late to erase the memory of the degrading event itself, but not too late to erase the prior day's attempt to erase the event. Thus, with no memory of taking the roofie, but the memory of the event very much alive, the victim of the roofie circle finds himself constantly trying to re-erase the memory...
jokes.setup: The narrator explains the 'roofie circle' concept as Gob's montage plays.
jokes.punchline: The formal, academic tone of the narrator explaining an increasingly absurd loop — complete with 'whereby' and logical sub-clauses — applied to Gob's self-inflicted drug cycle, ending in a Mexican hospital with stage-four syphilis.
jokes.explanation: The 'roofie circle' explanation is a masterpiece of AD narrator comedy. The mock-academic formality ('whereby,' 'thus') applied to an escalating self-destructive loop — punctuated by seasonal indicators (Christmas decorations), relationship deterioration ('Hey, remember me?'), and the final destination of 'stage-four syphilis in a Mexican hospital' — is exceptional comedy writing. The concept itself is original and the execution is near-flawless. This justifies its high scores.

jokes.text: Can I get a picture with him to show my five-year-old? You have a five-year-old? A granddaughter.
jokes.setup: A fan asks for a photo with Mark Cherry to show to their five-year-old.
jokes.punchline: The 'five-year-old' turns out to be a granddaughter — implying the fan is much older than the target demographic, subverting the expectation that a pop star's fans are young.
jokes.explanation: A clean misdirection joke — the 'five-year-old' as grandchild is a quick subversion — but not particularly inventive or character-specific. Functional comedy.

jokes.text: ♪ Go away, Getaway, stay away, Getaway... ♪ But the lyrics were too subtle for Gob to notice.
jokes.setup: Mark Cherry writes a song about Gob to get him to leave.
jokes.punchline: The song is literally called 'Getaway' and says 'go away' — the least subtle possible message — and Gob doesn't notice because the lyrics are 'too subtle for him.'
jokes.explanation: The irony of labeling extremely blunt song lyrics as 'too subtle for Gob to notice' is beautifully constructed. Gob's obliviousness has been established throughout, and the narrator's deadpan description of the blatantly unsubtle as 'too subtle' is a great joke about his inability to process social signals. Will recur as a running gag.

jokes.text: He got a call that his bee colony wasn't doing much better from a very much alive Johnny Bark.
jokes.setup: Gob receives bad news about his bees.
jokes.punchline: 'From a very much alive Johnny Bark' — the 'very much alive' qualifier implies that there was some reason to believe Johnny Bark might not be alive, played as a casual aside.
jokes.explanation: AD's characteristic trick of inserting reassuring qualifiers ('very much alive') that create more questions than they answer. Works as a quick callback/running-gag callback for fans of the earlier seasons.

jokes.text: They're sick. They're going to collapse the whole colony. Did you tell him about the sick bees? Jesus, I just said that!
jokes.setup: Johnny Bark reports on Gob's sick bees.
jokes.punchline: Johnny Bark's irritated 'Jesus, I just said that!' — implying Gob immediately asked if someone told him the thing he literally just heard — reveals Gob's inability to process information.
jokes.explanation: A good character beat showing Gob's attention deficiency, but relatively simple compared to the episode's better jokes.

jokes.text: Guys, hurry before he sees us. Come on. Hey, guys, where we going? I'll drive, I just got to put my sick bees in the trunk.
jokes.setup: The entourage tries to sneak out without Gob.
jokes.punchline: Gob catches them but misreads the situation entirely — offering to drive while casually mentioning his sick bees in the trunk as if this is a normal logistical addendum to a night out.
jokes.explanation: 'I just got to put my sick bees in the trunk' as a casual rider condition for a night out is great character comedy — Gob's complete obliviousness to social dynamics, combined with the absurdist detail of the bees.

jokes.text: You know what'd be really cool? If you shut the partition. It'd be like a joke, like you're our limo driver... That would be hilarious, right?
jokes.setup: Gob is in the limo with the entourage.
jokes.punchline: Mark Cherry asks Gob to shut the partition as a joke — because he IS their limo driver, he's been driving them all along — and treats degrading Gob as comedy fodder while Gob doesn't realize the joke is at his expense.
jokes.explanation: The cringe is well-executed — Gob is literally being told to go back to his limo-driver position while being framed as a joke, and he can't see it. Good sustained discomfort comedy.

jokes.text: A lot of traffic out here today, Miss Daisy.
jokes.setup: Gob has shut the partition and is driving the entourage.
jokes.punchline: Gob plays the driver character to the hilt — including a 'Driving Miss Daisy' reference — seemingly unaware that he is, in reality, their actual driver and this is not a performance.
jokes.explanation: Gob committing to the driver bit — 'Miss Daisy' — while literally being the driver is a solid irony beat. The self-unawareness is the joke.

jokes.text: For Gob, however, it was an amazing night, and he couldn't wait to forget it.
jokes.setup: The entourage is drunken, sick, and bee-stung after the night — a disaster.
jokes.punchline: 'He couldn't wait to forget it' — an ironic inversion, but also literally true (he will take a forget-me-now), and the contrast between his 'amazing night' assessment and the chaos around him.
jokes.explanation: A masterful double-meaning line: 'couldn't wait to forget it' is both ironic (it was objectively terrible) and literally true (Gob will drug himself). The economy is excellent and it ties the roofie circle narrative together beautifully.

jokes.text: Fun night. A fun night. Fun night. [Three separate beats across time, shown in sequence]
jokes.setup: The forget-me-now loop is visualized through Gob repeating 'Fun night' at different times.
jokes.punchline: The triple repetition of 'Fun night' — each in a different context/time — visualizes the roofie circle: Gob keeps forgetting and re-experiencing the same shame, each time resetting to 'Fun night' as his new baseline.
jokes.explanation: The visual structure of the 'Fun night' loop is exceptional — three identical words carrying exponential comedic weight through repetition across time. It's both a joke about Gob and a brilliant formal device showing the audience the roofie circle in action rather than explaining it. Near-exceptional execution.

jokes.text: Gob had slept through the seven ambulances that had arrived after Josh Abramson had swung by to pick up an old computer.
jokes.setup: The morning after the bee-sting incident.
jokes.punchline: Seven ambulances — presumably for bee-stung entourage members — casually noted as something Gob slept through, with the cause ('picking up an old computer') implying Josh Abramson was also stung while on a mundane errand.
jokes.explanation: Seven ambulances as a casualty count — treated as a throwaway detail — is great dark deadpan. The cause ('picking up an old computer') makes Josh's victimhood absurdly mundane.

jokes.text: It was then that Gob noticed the presence of a competitor from his past. T.W. Tony Wonder.
jokes.setup: Gob is at the Opies award show.
jokes.punchline: 'T.W. Tony Wonder' — the narrator giving Tony Wonder a formal initials title as if he's a dignitary, simultaneously a joke about the pomposity of the magic world and about how Gob perceives rivals.
jokes.explanation: A quick, functional joke about the pretension of the magic world. Not particularly inventive but serves a narrative function.

jokes.text: And that's when Gob decided to take advantage of an unexpected diversion and have a little fun by wedging shut a panel on the podium he knew Tony Wonder would pop out of.
jokes.setup: Gob spots Tony Wonder's trick setup at the Opies.
jokes.punchline: Gob sabotages Tony Wonder's act as casual 'fun' — treating professional sabotage as a light prank — setting up the payoff of Tony Wonder's later successful escape despite Gob's interference.
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the Tony Wonder payoff. The casual framing of sabotage as 'fun' is character-specific to Gob. The rewatch bonus comes from knowing the irony of the season finale implications.

jokes.text: Well, it's just... A little alcohol poisoning never killed anyone. Actually, alcohol poisoning is responsible for over 4,000 deaths a year. Binge drinking, not cool.
jokes.setup: Gob minimizes Mark Cherry's alcohol poisoning hospitalization.
jokes.punchline: An in-episode PSA interrupts Gob's rationalizing to directly contradict him with statistics, treating his dialogue as the setup for a factual correction.
jokes.explanation: The in-episode PSA format is a good structural joke — Gob's casual dismissal of alcohol poisoning immediately corrected by an authoritative counter — and the 'Binge drinking, not cool' tagline is amusingly clunky as PSA messaging.

jokes.text: I'm here, I'm queer. And now I'm in a speaker.
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder emerges from a speaker instead of the wedged podium at the Opies.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm here, I'm queer, and now I'm in a speaker' — a riff on the activist chant 'We're here, we're queer, get used to it,' adapted for his gay magician persona, landing as both identity-politics parody and magic punchline.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. The riff on the activist chant adapted to magic context — 'and now I'm in a speaker' as the third beat — is a brilliantly constructed three-part structure. The specificity and economy make this exceptional. Perfect payoff to the Tony Wonder setup.

jokes.text: ♪ Go away, Getaway, stay away, Getaway... ♪ Great, and now my boss is up my ass.
jokes.setup: Mark Cherry's song plays as Gob loses everything at once.
jokes.punchline: The song plays — now an ironic anthem — and Gob's only response is a complaint about his boss (as if the 'boss' relationship with Mark Cherry is his primary concern in this moment of total loss).
jokes.explanation: The 'Getaway' song has now accumulated enough repetition to land as a running-gag punchline. Gob calling Mark Cherry 'my boss' is the self-revelation of how far he's fallen.

jokes.text: My cave. [Gob finds his boulder/escape cave in a storage unit] Why didn't you open? Only to discover that someone had wedged shut his secret compartment on the day he deserted his Christian bride. 'T.' Tony Wonder.
jokes.setup: Gob investigates his old escape boulder, trying to understand why it didn't open at the wedding.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Tony Wonder sabotaged Gob's escape mechanism — 'T. Tony Wonder' — mirrors Gob's own sabotage of Tony Wonder at the Opies, while also explaining the entire catastrophic wedding plotline retroactively.
jokes.explanation: The episode's crown jewel. The final reveal that Tony Wonder sabotaged Gob's boulder — just as Gob sabotaged Tony's podium — is a perfect structural callback that (1) explains the entire plot, (2) mirrors Gob's own behavior in the same episode, (3) sets up the Tony Wonder rivalry arc for the season, and (4) rewards viewers who remembered Gob's casual sabotage earlier. The structure (9.5) is among the best in the episode — this is what AD does at its finest: a comedic payoff that simultaneously recontextualizes everything before it. Easily justifies 9+ scores.

tmdb_overview: Gob finds a new group of friends after his relationship falls apart and his family abandons him.
insight: Buster's regression accelerates: 1.74 jokes per minute sustains the season's darkest character arc.
